product_overview: ZTBrowser is a developer trust platform that lets a browser verify an enclave-backed execution claim, enrich that claim with public provenance metadata, and support repeatable deployment of attested services.

general_info.name: Browser-visible attestation verification
general_info.overview: The extension fetches attestation from the current origin, verifies the attestation document, and shows a lock or failure reason.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Give users and operators a browser-visible trust decision for enclave-backed services.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Attestation is usually backend-oriented and difficult to verify from the browser.

actors_and_roles.secondary_user: End user consuming a trust-marked site

business_rules.description: The lock is shown only after successful cryptographic verification and nonce match.
Facts lookup may enrich metadata but must never override a cryptographic verification failure.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want the extension to show a lock only when the attestation is valid, so that I do not trust unverifiable services.

bdd.given: A page origin exposes a valid Nitro attestation endpoint
and_preconditions: The extension can fetch the endpoint
The trusted root set contains the required root certificate
bdd.when: The extension validates the returned attestation document for the generated nonce
bdd.then: The extension stores workingEnv=true and codeValidated=true and shows a locked state
and_postconditions: Verified PCRs are available for optional facts lookup

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want invalid or malformed attestation to remain unlocked with a reason, so that failures are visible rather than silent.

bdd.given: A page origin returns an invalid or malformed attestation response
bdd.when: The verifier detects invalid signature, invalid chain, bad payload, or unsupported platform
bdd.then: The extension stores an unlocked state and a structured failure reason

non_functional_requirements.description: Verification must happen inside the extension runtime or another trusted local component, not in the remote page context.
Failure reasons must be inspectable through the popup and debug trace.

test_cases.setup: Load a valid attestation document and matching nonce

general_info.name: Facts and provenance lookup
general_info.overview: The extension maps checker-verified PCR tuples to public workload and release metadata.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Explain what measured workload identity the browser has verified.
goal_and_context.user_problem: A valid attestation document alone does not tell users which repo or release the service corresponds to.

business_rules.description: Facts lookup is keyed by verified PCR tuple, not by repo name or user input.
A facts miss must not invalidate a successful cryptographic verification result.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want a verified PCR tuple to resolve to workload metadata when available, so that I can understand what was measured.

bdd.given: The verifier has already produced PCR values
and_preconditions: The facts node contains a matching row
bdd.then: The extension stores factsMatched=true and workload metadata
and_postconditions: The popup can show repo URL and image digest

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a user, I want the extension to stay locked even when facts lookup misses, so that metadata outages do not falsify cryptographic verification.

bdd.given: The verifier has produced a successful verdict but the facts node has no matching row or is unavailable
bdd.then: The extension keeps the cryptographic success state and records factsMatched=false
and_postconditions: The popup may show no workload metadata

non_functional_requirements.description: Facts rows must be treated as transparency metadata, not as a cryptographic root of trust.
Facts schema must support canonical release provenance fields without breaking lookup-by-pcr.

test_cases.setup: Provide verified PCRs and a matching facts row
Provide verified PCRs and an unavailable or mismatching facts service
test_cases.assertion: lock remains based on verification and factsMatched=false

general_info.name: Canonical enclave release provenance
general_info.overview: The measured workload is owned by a dedicated enclave repo that publishes canonical artifacts and facts updates.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Make runtime measurement claims auditable and reproducible.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Manual workspace builds and monorepo-level identity make PCR claims ambiguous and hard to audit.

business_rules.description: Canonical facts rows must come from tagged enclave release CI, not from manual local builds.
The measured enclave source is the dedicated enclave repo, not the entire integration monorepo.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want a tagged enclave release to generate canonical artifacts and a facts PR, so that the deployed identity is traceable.

bdd.given: A release tag is pushed in the canonical enclave repo
bdd.then: The EIF, measurements, provenance manifest, and facts-row update are produced
and_postconditions: A PR can update canonical facts in ztbrowser

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a reviewer, I want a rebuild-verification path, so that I can compare published measurements against an independent rebuild.

bdd.when: The same repo and ref are rebuilt
bdd.then: The EIF and measurement outputs can be compared against the published manifest

non_functional_requirements.description: Canonical provenance must record release tag, commit SHA, PCRs, and artifact hashes.
Third parties must be able to rerun a documented rebuild-verification process.

test_cases.setup: Trigger a tagged release in the enclave repo
test_cases.assertion: Artifacts and facts-row update are generated

test_cases.setup: Use provenance.json and the rebuild verifier
test_cases.action: Rebuild the enclave repo at the tagged ref

general_info.name: Operator deployment workflow
general_info.overview: Operators can deploy and verify the canonical workload using AWS CLI automation and a TUI.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Reduce deployment friction and make attested deployment operations observable.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Manual cloud console deployment is slow, error-prone, and difficult to demonstrate or repeat.

business_rules.description: A successful verify run must check landing page and attestation endpoint before reporting success.
Verify runs clean up by default; deploy runs keep the environment live.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want a verify action that deploys, checks the service, and cleans up automatically, so that I can validate the path without leaving infrastructure running.

bdd.given: The AWS profile and release tag are configured
bdd.when: The operator runs the verify flow
bdd.then: The workload is deployed, verified, and then stopped or terminated based on cleanup settings

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As an operator, I want to inspect and manage running deployments from the TUI, so that live instances remain visible and controllable.

bdd.then: The UI lists instance IDs, states, instance types, and public IPs and allows stop/terminate actions
and_postconditions: Operators can manage lifecycle without leaving the TUI

non_functional_requirements.description: Deployment stages must expose live logs and status rather than one opaque command.
Cleanup defaults must avoid leaving infrastructure running unintentionally.

test_cases.setup: Configure verify action and cleanup mode
test_cases.assertion: Landing page and attestation checks succeed before cleanup

test_cases.setup: Have at least one managed instance present
test_cases.assertion: Instance metadata and lifecycle actions are visible

general_info.name: Demo and self-signed application path
general_info.overview: Micrus and the demo service let teams exercise the trust flow with a self-signed root or a Nitro-shaped simulation path.

goal_and_context.business_goal: Provide a low-friction educational and product-demo path before a canonical enclave rollout is required.
goal_and_context.user_problem: Teams need a realistic demo path without always performing a full AWS Nitro deployment.

business_rules.description: Self-signed demo paths must be clearly distinguished from canonical AWS Nitro verification.
Demo trust roots can support learning and product prototyping, but they must not be presented as canonical provenance.

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a demo operator, I want Micrus to verify under a demo trust root, so that I can present an application-facing trust flow locally.

bdd.given: Micrus is running and the demo root is configured as trusted
and_preconditions: The extension or checker trusts the demo root
bdd.then: The trust decision succeeds under the demo keychain
and_postconditions: The UI can identify the demo trust root

acceptance_criteria.user_story: As a presenter, I want the canonical AWS path and the self-signed demo path to remain clearly separated, so that the audience understands which guarantees differ.

bdd.given: Both canonical and demo flows exist in the project
and_preconditions: Documentation and UI expose trust-root context
bdd.then: The demo root is shown as noncanonical and the canonical AWS path remains distinct
and_postconditions: No false provenance claim is made

non_functional_requirements.description: The UX must distinguish trusted-root context such as AWS root versus demo root.
Demo flows must not require architectural compromises in the canonical release path.

test_cases.setup: Run Micrus with demo PKI and trust the demo root
test_cases.assertion: The verifier accepts the demo chain and exposes demo trust-root context
Popup distinguishes demo keychain from canonical AWS keychain
